items.q: Why is ATP described as an 'energy currency' rather than energy storage?
items.correct: ATP is constantly made and broken down — it transfers energy between reactions rather than storing large amounts for later.
items.misconception: Cells store large amounts of ATP for emergency use.
items.random_wrong: ATP is permanent and accumulates with age in cells.
items.why: Students treat ATP as a battery that fills up and gets used; in reality, cells make ATP just-in-time and have only seconds' worth at any moment.

items.q: What is actually transferred to the body when you get a vaccine?
items.correct: Antigens (often from weakened, dead, or fragmented pathogens) that train the immune system to make memory cells.
items.misconception: Vaccines inject ready-made antibodies directly into the bloodstream.
items.random_wrong: Vaccines insert new genes into your DNA permanently.
items.why: Students confuse vaccines (which prompt your own immune response) with passive immunity (which delivers ready-made antibodies, e.g. antivenom).

items.q: What is the purpose of the refractory period after an action potential?
items.correct: It ensures action potentials travel in one direction only and limits the maximum firing frequency.
items.misconception: The refractory period exists because the cell runs out of energy and needs a rest.
items.random_wrong: The refractory period blocks all signals from reaching the neurone.
items.why: Students think of it as a 'rest break' rather than a functional feature ensuring unidirectional propagation.
The answer examiners want is: Animal that hunts others. “Plant that makes food” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Increase. “Decrease” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: There are 200 rabbits and predators eat 30 per month. How many left after 1 month?
items.why: The real figure is 170. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
